title: Cat Rectal Prolapse: Help for Red Tissue Protruding from Rectum
meta_desc: If your cat has red tissue protruding from their rectum, it is a medical emergency. Learn why rectal prolapse happens and what you should do now.
content_html:
What is Rectal Prolapse?

Rectal prolapse occurs when one or more layers of the rectum protrude through the anal opening. This typically appears as a cylindrical, tube-like mass of red or pink tissue. It is most often caused by excessive straining, which can result from severe constipation, diarrhea, or urinary tract issues.

Is This a Veterinary Emergency?

Urgency Level: High. Yes, this is an immediate veterinary emergency. When rectal tissue remains outside the body, it quickly loses moisture and blood supply. Without rapid treatment, the tissue can become necrotic (die) or severely infected. Do not attempt to push the tissue back in yourself, as you may cause internal tearing or permanent damage to the sphincter muscle.

Why Taking a Photo Helps Triage

While you are preparing to transport your cat to the clinic, taking a clear photo of the protrusion can be vital. Sharing this photo with your veterinarian allows them to assess the severity, color, and size of the prolapse instantly. This helps the medical team prepare the necessary surgical or sedative tools before you even walk through the door, saving precious time for your pet.

faqs.question: Can I treat a cat's rectal prolapse at home?
faqs.answer: No. Rectal prolapse requires professional medical intervention to address the protruding tissue and identify the underlying cause of the straining to prevent recurrence.

faqs.question: What should I do while driving to the vet?
faqs.answer: Keep your cat calm and prevent them from licking or biting the area. You can apply a small amount of water-based lubricant or a cool, damp cloth to the exposed tissue to keep it moist, but do not apply pressure.

faqs.question: What are common causes of rectal prolapse in cats?
faqs.answer: Common causes include intestinal parasites (worms), chronic constipation, inflammatory bowel disease, or urinary obstructions that lead to intense straining.
